coredata.dc:description: Diabetes is a chronic metabolic disease that lasts a person's life and the estimation of blood glucose concentration is one of the main diagnostic criteria for this disease. The past decade has witnessed a huge demand for new biocompatibility and high-performance glucose biosensors. Non-enzymatic optical glucose sensing using nano/microscaled materials has surged significantly as it provides a direct visualized and cost-effective way to measure glucose concentrations. This review, in a non-exhaustive way, considers some of the recent most important achievements in the glucose-sensitive optical biosensors based upon nano/microscaled materials. Such materials are capable of mimicking the activities of the enzymes which oxidize glucose to release H2O2. The overall framework of the review can be divided into three parts. The first part describes the colorimetric glucose sensing using nano/microscaled materials as enzyme mimics for the oxidation of different chromogenic substrates. In the second part, some recent developments in chromogenic substrate-free fluorescence glucose sensing facilitated by nano/microscaled materials are elaborated. The third part reviews the glucose-sensing through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) based assays. Finally, some current challenges, gaps in fundamental understanding and future improvements in the field of optical glucose biosensors are discussed.

head.ce:title: Nano/micro-scaled materials based optical biosensing of glucose

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diabetes is a chronic metabolic disease that lasts a person's life and the estimation of blood glucose concentration is one of the main diagnostic criteria for this disease. The past decade has witnessed a huge demand for new biocompatibility and high-performance glucose biosensors. Non-enzymatic optical glucose sensing using nano/microscaled materials has surged significantly as it provides a direct visualized and cost-effective way to measure glucose concentrations. This review, in a non-exhaustive way, considers some of the recent most important achievements in the glucose-sensitive optical biosensors based upon nano/microscaled materials. Such materials are capable of mimicking the activities of the enzymes which oxidize glucose to release H2O2. The overall framework of the review can be divided into three parts. The first part describes the colorimetric glucose sensing using nano/microscaled materials as enzyme mimics for the oxidation of different chromogenic substrates. In the second part, some recent developments in chromogenic substrate-free fluorescence glucose sensing facilitated by nano/microscaled materials are elaborated. The third part reviews the glucose-sensing through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) based assays. Finally, some current challenges, gaps in fundamental understanding and future improvements in the field of optical glucose biosensors are discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes mellitus caused by insufficient insulin production due to the destruction of pancreatic β-cells (Type-1), excess production and ineffective use of insulin (Type-II) and gestational diabetes (Hyperglycaemia) is the seventh leading cause of deaths in the world [1]. It is estimated that by 2035, the number of people affected will reach 592 million, 642 million by 2040 and 693 million by 2045 [2–4]. The need of the hour is thus to design and engineer fast, reliable and highly sensitive glucose sensors for real-time monitoring of physiological glucose. Up to now, to serve the purpose of quantitative glucose detection, various detection methods based on optical [5,6], electrochemical [7,8], capillary zone electrophoresis [9], high-performance liquid chromatography [10], etc. have been applied. Currently, invasive self-monitored finger-prick type blood glucose sensors are commercially used. These sensors amperometrically detect enzymatically released H2O2 during oxidation of blood glucose to gluconic acid within a quick response times of ∼5 s and a dynamic glucose-sensing range of 10.81–600 mg/dl [11,12]. However, this method is painful, inconvenient and prone to infection, especially for patients who need to check their blood sugar levels multiple times a day.

The latest developments in the field of nanotechnology have opened up many ways for the diagnosis of many terrible diseases. The compelling properties and functionality of nano/microscaled materials make them ideal for end-user applications for the development of cutting-edge technologies such as electrochemical sensors [13–15], biosensors [16–18], gas sensors [19], antimicrobial agents [20,21], supercapacitors [22], electronics [23], photocatalysts [24–27], optical sensors [28–30], electromagnetism [31], dye-sensitized solar cells [32], etc.

Recently, the research activities in optical glucose sensing have surged significantly and the fabrication of glucose biosensors have been one of the major implementations of nano/microscaled materials. The optical glucose sensing based upon nano/microscaled materials provides a direct visualized and cost-effective convenient method to measure glucose concentrations as compared to electrochemical glucose sensing which needs expensive and involuted instrumentation, typical conditions for the fabrication of sensor devices, maintaining operational conditions and deployment of professionally skilled technicians. Such biosensors have also been improved for the key sensor properties related to high sensitivity, selectivity, long-term stability, the limit of detection, accuracy, precision and reusability.

Considering the practical application of optical sensors, techniques such as Raman spectroscopy [33,34], fluorescence spectroscopy [35], near-infrared (NIR) spectroscopy [36], dynamic light scattering [37], surface plasmons resonance (SPR) [38,39], Fourier transforms near-infrared (FT-NIRS) [40], nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy [41], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) spectroscopy [42] and optical coherence tomography (OCT) [43] etc. have been explored. The basic simple principle of these optical methods is based on the change of scattered light intensity from the surface of the molecular recognition compounds i.e. nano/microscaled materials due to their interactions with the glucose molecules [44]. These techniques also offer direct, label-free, and real-time fast detection of glucose [45]. Additionally, many nano/microstructured materials have been found to mimic the horseradish peroxidase activities and result in the release of H2O2 during non-enzymatic oxidation of glucose. The released H2O2 can easily oxidize a variety of colorless chromogenic substrates to their colored oxidized forms [46]. Nonenzymatic glucose sensing is considered to be better as compared to enzymatic one since the latter involves the use of natural enzymes that have their own limitations including difficulty in purification, facile denaturation, being expensive, highly unstable and so on [47]. Surface-to-volume ratio, composition, crystal structure and phase, morphology and the presence of conducting matrices in nano/microstructured materials are the key factors that significantly affect the nonenzymatic optical glucose sensing applications [48,49].

Recently pulished reviews reported the use of various nano/microstructures as a mimic for peroxidase enzyme for the oxidation of different chromogenic substrates by direct use of H2O2 through colorimetric methods [50,51]. However, very little has been reviewed for the indirect optical estimation and sensing of glucose which also releases H2O2 during its enzymatic oxidation to gluconic acid by glucose oxidase. Since diabetes is a metabolic disease with the characteristics of metabolic disorders and hyperglycemia, it can cause chronic damage and dysfunction of various tissues of the human body, so it is important to detect glucose concentrations. Therefore, in this review, various nano/microscaled materials have been reviewed which mimic peroxidase-like activities. The mechanisms of the various optical techniques used for glucose sensing have also been illustrated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The basic chemistry of colorimetric sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
The most commonly reported chromogenic substrates for the detection of H2O2 and glucose are 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), phenol/4‐aminoantipyrine (4‐AAP) and 2,2-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS). H2O2 can oxidize colorless TMB to blue colored oxidized TMB (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine diimine) with λ max ∼655 nm [52], while for ABTS, the color changes from slightly yellow to an intensely turquoise (blue/green) colored solution (λ
max
. = 405 nm). These transformations are achieved by the presence of horseradish peroxidase. It is a well-known fact that glucose oxidase (GOD) is capable of oxidizing glucose to gluconolactone. Another less commonly used chromogenic substrate for the quantification of horseradish peroxidase is o-phenylenediamine (OPD). It is a colorless and nonfluorescent substance which in the presence of horseradish peroxidase and H2O2 is oxidized to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) which is colored as well as fluorescent with λ
max
. = 417 nm (Eq. (1)).
Image 1(1)(1)

Despite biocompatibility, substrate speciﬁcity and high catalytic efficiency, enzyme-based biosensors still face some of the major intrinsic disadvantages associated with enzymes, such as cumbersome separation and purification methods, temperature and pH sensitivity leading to denaturation, poor stability, inactivation, humidity sensitivity, uneconomical, non-recyclable, non-reusable, etc. Recent research suggests that all of these problems can be overcome by using nanomaterials as biomimetics and substitute for enzymes. Nano/microscaled materials not only provide high-density adsorption sites for adsorbing analyte materials but also act as catalysts for biochemical reactions. Metals and metal oxides can be used as signal transducers and/or target recognition elements for biosensors. The transducer properties can further be enhanced or improved by making the composites of the metal nanoparticles with other conducting materials including single or multi-walled carbon nanotubes, graphene materials (graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide, graphene), carbon paste, etc. TMB with basic amine groups is easily protonated under acidic conditions and thus is strongly attracted towards negatively charged nano/microstructures. However, these attractions further depend upon the nature of the functional groups of the capping agents around the nanoparticle's surface. ABTS contains negatively charged sulfonic groups (-SO3 –), and thus shows a strong affinity toward positively charged nano/microstructures. Compared to TNB, ABTS shows relatively better results under physiological conditions. According to Liu et al. [53], the negatively charged poly-(sodium 4-styrenesulfonate) capped Au@Pt nanorods yielded a higher affinity to TMB, while the positively charged poly (diallyldimethyl ammonium chloride) nanorods exhibited a relatively lower affinity. In contrast, for ABTS, the opposite trends were observed. A two-step indirect colorimetric method can be proposed for the sensing and detection of glucose. The initial step involves the enzymatic glucose to gluconolactone of gluconic acid conversion (Eqs. 2,3) generally at physiological conditions i.e. pH 7.4 and temperature 37 °C and the final step oxidation of colorless TMB to blue colored TMB. Higher the concentration of the glucose higher is the formation of H2O2 and thus greater is the oxidation of TMB to TMB oxide. H2O2 is easily adsorbed on the surface of the nano/microstructures where hemolytic cleavage of O–O to generate hydroxyl free radicals (OH•). The partial electron exchange interaction in the case of metals and the n-type or p-type semiconducting nature of metal compounds stabilize the generated OH• and thus contribute towards the catalytic oxidation of the TMB (Fig. 1 ).
Image 2(2)
Image 3(3)

Original color responses of chromogenic substrates OPD, TMB, ABTS by the action of H2O2 in the presence of Au@Pt nanorods as peroxidase mimic is shown in Fig. 2 (a). The corresponding related mechanism for the oxidation of OPD to DAP is depicted in Fig. 2(b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric glucose biosensing with oxidizable chromogenic agents

ce:section.ce:para: The AuNPs can easily substitute and mimic the enzyme activities; in particular, peroxidase and such characteristics can be further promoted by different surface modifications. Negatively and positively charged Au nanoparticles were obtained using citrate and cysteamine as capping agents, respectively. Jv et al. [54] reported positively charged Au nanoparticles [(+)AuNPs] synthesized by the reduction of hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III) by NaBH4 in the presence of cysteamine which incorporates the positively charged –NH3 + groups. The average size and the zeta potential of the as-synthesized nanoparticles were 34 nm and +11.3 mV, respectively. Further, (+) AuNPs with an average particle size of 34 nm exhibited better catalytic oxidation of TMB than the (+) AuNPs with an average particle size of 48 nm due to greater surface-to-volume ratio of former than later. Citrate-capped (-) AuNPs with 13 nm size also exhibited higher catalytic activity than (-) AuNPs having size 38 nm. Jiang et al. [46] prepared positively charged chitosan-functionalized AuNPs. The AuNPs were stabilized by the electrostatic forces of attraction between amino groups in chitosan and AuCl4 − precursor. These positively charged chitosan-functionalized AuNPs exhibited very low LOD of 3 mM and 12 mM, respectively in an analytical sample and 60% serum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bimetallic Au NP composites with other noble metals
ce:section.ce:para: Bimetallic nanoparticles have attracted much attention because of their better catalytic performance than their single metal counterparts along with their ability to systematically adjust their composition, coordination environment, electrical and electronic states [55,56]. Bimetallic Au-Ni doped graphitic carbon nitride sheets (Au-Ni/g-C3N4) synthesized by Darabdhara et al. [56] exhibited better sensing results than monometallic Au/g-C3N4 and Ni/g-C3N4 sensors as a mimic for peroxidase during colorimetric glucose sensing. The LOD for Au-Ni/g-C3N4, Au/g-C3N4 and Ni/g-C3N4 were 1.7 μM, 3.55 μM and 6.28 μM, respectively (Fig. 4 (a)). The synergistic effect accounts from the electronic charge transfer between Au and Ni. Due to comparative ionization potentials (Au = 9.22 eV and Ni = 7.63 eV), fast electron transfer from Ni to Au atoms increases the electron charge density for Au-Ni/g-C3N4 composites as compared to individual metallic components [57]. Interaction between Au and Ni is further supported by g-C3N4 which also contributes to the delocalized electrons donated from its lone pairs to the metallic components. g-C3N4 also increases the surface area and hence provides a good network for electron mobilization and a large extent of adsorption of analyte molecules and TMB [58,59]. This fast immobilization of electrons helps the Au-Ni/g-C3N4 composites to generate OH• free radicals which were confirmed by photoluminescence studies. Since glucose oxidase is highly specific in nature and can catalyze the oxidation of glucose only, other sugars responded poorly during the colorimetric sensing despite having similar lone pairs of electrons and comparative molecular dimensions as glucose (Fig. 4 (b) and (c)). Recently, synergistic coupling catalysis from GO/AuNPs nanocomposites, prepared hydrothermally, was analyzed by Qi et al. [60] for the colorimetric sensing of glucose. Over a concentration range of 5.1 × 10−6 – 5.1 × 10−4 M, excellent linearity at 655 nm was observed. The detection limit was measured to be 6.3 × 10−7 M.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Composites of monometallic or bimetallic nano/micro-materials
ce:section.ce:para: To prevent agglomeration and improve the environmental stability of the metal nanoparticles, economical and more stable alternatives have been explored. Porphyrins, supramolecules, biomolecules, polymers, carbonaceous materials and metal complexes have been used as matrices to incorporate metallic nanoparticles [61]. Such matrices not only functionalize the metallic nanoparticles to accelerate the electron transfer processes but also provide high adsorption capacities for the reactant substrates and target analytes.

The creative studies utilizing metal oxide nanostructures as inherent mimetic enzymes recently have opened multiple entryways for the advancement of nano-scaled inorganic materials in a variety of biochemical fields. Metal oxides nanostructures such as Fe3O4 [68,69], ZnO, CeO2 [70], CuO [71], TiO2 [70], WO3, Co3O4, Fe2O3 [72] and many more have been reported as potential horseradish peroxidase, glucose oxidase, or glucose dehydrogenase enzyme mimetics being applied in biosensing applications for glucose, H2O2 and other monosaccharides colorimetrically in the presence of different chromogenic substrates.

Iron oxides, particularly ferrite (Fe3O4) magnetic nanoparticles show better binding tendencies for chromogenic substrates than horseradish peroxidase. Further, these materials possess a 40-fold higher level of activity as compared to horseradish peroxidase [73]. Fe3+ ions of the Fe3O4 have shown the tendency of the conversion of H2O2, released by the oxidation of glucose to gluconic acid, into OH• radicals (Eqs. (4)–(9)) [74–76]. The fact is supported by the continuous increase in the absorbance of the oxidized TMB with the increased amount of the metal oxide Fe2O3.

The generation of OH• radical was also confirmed by terephthalic acid photoluminescence probes wherein non-luminescent terephthalic acid reacts with OH• to produce strong luminescent due to the formation of 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid [77].

Hybrid composite materials have also been widely utilized to realize the combination of the characteristics of individual components to achieve synergistically enhanced functions. Incorporation of other components like carboxyl-modified graphene oxide, capable of catalyzing the oxidation of TMB to blue-colored oxidized TMB, along with metal oxides matrix suggests better sensing behavior for glucose [68,78]. Further, compared to peroxidase and metal oxide nanoparticles, carboxylated GO-COOH possesses high surface-to-volume ratio as well as a high affinity to organic substrates through π–π and hydrophobic interactions. Additionally, the adsorption of the TMB on the surface of graphene transfers a pair of electrons thereby increasing the electron density and mobility in graphene. This in turn raises the Fermi level energy which favors accelerated electron transfer from graphene to H2O2 [79].

Exceptionally, metal oxides with mixed valances such as Fe3O4 are highly favorable for sensing applications due to the presence of Fe2+/3+ redox couples. In this regard, many research groups explored the glucose-sensing properties of the carbonaceous materials-Fe3O4 composites. In addition to graphene oxide, other carbonaceous materials like multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) [80], graphite [81], 3D porous graphene [69], carbon nitride [82], etc. are also utilized as fillers for metal oxides in glucose biosensing. Dong et al. [68] found the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity with a very high affinity for H2O2 for graphene oxide–Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposites. It was observed that the nanocomposites smartly retained the magnetic properties which helped in the effective separation of the catalytic composites after the sensing procedure. The linear dynamic glucose concentration range and limit of detection were 1–200 μM and 0.74 μM, respectively. Magnetic Fe3O4/MWCNTs prepared by in situ assemblies of Fe3O4 on MWCNTs nanostructures by chemical reduction with hydrazine acted better peroxidase mimetics as was revealed by higher rates of phenol/4‐aminoantipyrine oxidation [80]. The synergistic effect of Fe3O4 and MWCNTs resulted in enhanced peroxidase enzyme‐like activities. Phenol/4‐aminoantipyrine is oxidized to colored quinone imine dye in the presence of H2O2 released from glucose oxidation to gluconolactone catalyzed by FAD-glucose oxidase. Further, in the presence of butyl‐imidazolium bromide ionic liquid oxidation rate of the phenol/4‐aminoantipyrine was much faster than in traditional phosphate buffer solution. Wang et al. [69] reported a novel one-pot strategy for the synthesis of 3D porous graphene hydrogel decorated with uniformly distributed Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Hemin was used as iron precursor (Fig. 6 (a)). A very low detection limit of 0.8 μM was obtained through the oxidation of TMB (Fig. 6 (b)). It was assumed that the highly porous graphene hydrogel avoided the restacking and agglomeration of graphene sheets and thus increased the collision probability of the active molecules with Fe3O4, resulting in improved nanozyme activities. Carbon coated magnetite nanoparticle (Fe3O4@C) having excellent water-dispersion and peroxidase-like activity showed a linear relationship between absorbance of oxidized TMB and glucose concentration in the range from 6 to 100 μM and detection limit of 2 μM (Fig. 6 (c, d)) [81].

Graphite-like carbon nitride (C3N4)-Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposites prepared through a chemical co-precipitation method showed a linear concentration range from 1 to 140 μM and limit of detection 0.25 μM, respectively for glucose through TMB oxidation. The results were attributed to the synergism between g-C3N4-a metal-free photoactive semiconductor material with a narrow band gap of 2.7 eV and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles [82]. Fe3O4@CeO2 yolk-shell nanocomposites were synthesized by Fei et al. [83] by growing CeO2 nanoparticles on the surface of Fe3O4 hollow nanoparticles. Enhanced colorimetric glucose bio-sensitivity by Fe3O4@CeO2 yolk-shell nanocomposites were observed than pristine Fe3O4 and CeO2 nanoparticles through the oxidation of chromogenic agent TMB (Fig. 7 (a)). The limit of detection was 21 μM for glucose (Fig. 7 (b, c)). The results were attributed to the synergistic effect of the coupling of catalytic activities of the elemental Fe3O4 and CeO2 nanoparticles.
Recently reported Fe3O4 based glucose biosensors are compared for their sensing parameters in Table 2 .

Nano-/microstructured ceria (CeO2), due to their fascinating properties like high specific surface areas, chemical inertness, non-toxicity, optical, chemiluminescence, biocompatible nature, wide band gap of 3.4 eV, high isoelectric point of 9.2 and high electron transfer capabilities, are widely explored as base materials for the fabrication of electrochemical and colorimetric biosensors [91–94]. The adsorption of the low isoelectric point enzymes such as glucose oxidase is favored on the surfaces with high isoelectric points [95,96]. Further, in CeO2, +3 and + 4 oxidation states coexist. The oxidation of the Ce3+ to Ce4+ additionally creates oxygen vacancies at the surface of the CeO2 [97]. For colorimetric biosensing of the glucose, the color changes are not significant in the case of CeO2 due to large crystalline size, low surface-to-volume ratio, and aggregation of the nanoparticles leading to mass transfer. To overcome these limitations a variety of schemes have been proposed [98]. Xu et al. [99] analyzed unique self-color transition by the H2O2-induced alteration of the oxidation states from Ce3+ to Ce4+ on the surface of the CeO2 nanoparticles. Kim et al. [100] proposed the synthesis of mesoporous CeO2 nanoparticles for reagent-free glucose biosensing. Excellent glucose biosensing parameters were attributed to a large number of highly ordered mesopores with 20 nm diameter which led to a facile accommodation of oxidative enzymes. Li et al. [101] reported hydrothermally synthesized coral-like CeO2 hollow nanostructures with excellent peroxidase mimic activities. The introduction of the NiO along with the Coral-like CeO2 in CeO2/NiO nanocomposites, however, showed better peroxidase mimicking [102]. In the presence of H2O2, Ce3+ is oxidized to Ce4+, as indicated by XPS studies, developed a color change from colorless to yellow/orange.

In addition to the above-mentioned metal oxides, some other metal oxide nanomaterials have also been reported as colorimetric biosensors using chromogenic agents. Graphene quantum dots-CuO nanoneedle composites were explored as perfect peroxidase mimics to catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 by Zhang et al. [71]. The peroxidase-like activity of the nanocomposites was attributed to the generation of OH• free radicals. Accurate detection of glucose with a linear range from 2 to 100 μM along with a detection limit of 0.59 μM was reported. Similar to graphene quantum dots-CuO nanoneedle composites, copper oxide-graphite carbon nitride nanocomposites have also been shown to exhibit peroxidase-mimicking activities [111]. Liu et al. [112] prepared 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(4-carboxyl phenyl)-porphyrin (H2TCPP) functionalized chain-like Co3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) and explored and compared their peroxidase-like activities with pure Co3O4 nanoparticles. NiO nanoparticles were also modified with H2TCPP for mimicking peroxidase-like activities for detecting H2O2 and glucose [113]. The glucose sensing parameters of some other metal oxide and composites such as human serum albumin templated MnO2 nanosheets [114], Green tide biomass templated synthesis of MoO nanorods supported on carbon [115], CoO assembled onto ordered-mesoporous carbon [116], V2O3-ordered mesoporous carbon composite [117], graphene oxide and CuO double quantum dot composites [118], CuO nanoparticles [119], Co3O4 nanoparticles [120], etc. are shown in Table 4 .

Similar to metal oxides, metal sulfide semiconducting materials have also been synthesized and explored for biosensing applications to spectrophotometrically analyze various biomarkers. Metal sulfide nano-/microstructures possess unique electrochemical, redox, optical and chemiluminescence properties, making them suitable substitutes for noble metals, metal oxides and carbonaceous materials in fabricating biosensors. Methods such as co-precipitation, hydrothermal, solvothermal, sol-gel, electro-deposition, microwave-assisted hydrothermal, chemical vapor deposition, etc. are reported to engineer the structural properties, surface defects, crystalline, optical and importantly the electrochemical redox properties of the metal sulfides. The best strategy involves the dissolution–recrystallization mechanism, converting, ‘oxide-to-sulfides’. Metal sulfides like MoS2, WS2, ZnS, MnS2, NiS2, CoS2, CdS, FeS2, SnS2, VS2, etc. have been reported for their biosensing behavior.

Among the various metal sulfides, MoS2 is widely reported for glucose biosensing as compared to other metal sulfides. Layered and non-layered sulfides are the two broadly classified categories. Layered two-dimensional MoS2 nano/micro-sheets consist of three atoms layered covalently bounded S–M–S arrangements held together through weak van der Waals forces of attraction. In contrast, non-layered MoS2 nano/microstructures consist of three-dimensional morphologies [123]. The bulk MoS2 has an indirect band gap of 1.2 eV whereas a single-layered MoS2 is a direct gap semiconductor with band gap energy of 1.8 eV. The electronic band energy in MoS2 can be engineered by the number of layers. In addition to fascinating optical features, great surface-area-to-volume ratio, high charge carrier mobility and low noise level also make MoS2 a promising building block for electrochemical and optical biosensors [124]. Lin et al. [125] claimed the absorbance peaks at 369 and 652 nm corresponding to oxidized TMB in the simultaneous presence of H2O2 and MoS2 nanosheets. The absorbance peaks were attributed to the charge-transfer complexes derived from the one-electron oxidation of TMB (oxTMB). However, this change was not observed if H2O2 or MoS2 were added separately. The absorption peak intensity was increased with the concentration of the H2O2. The results confirmed the peroxidase-like activities of the MoS2 nanosheets. This led to the fabrication of many MoS2 based H2O2 and glucose biosensors. For glucose, a low detection limit of 1.2 μM and linear dynamic concentration range of 5–150 μM was observed. The group also developed a simple, inexpensive, portable test kit for the visual detection of glucose in normal and diabetic serum samples using an agarose hydrogel visual detection platform. Cai et al. [126] analyzed the novel peroxidase mimic activities of MoS2- Pt74Ag26 nanohybrids synthesized by decorating ultrathin MoS2 nanosheets with octahedral Pt74Ag26 alloy nanoparticles. Linear concentration range and low detection limit of 1.0–10.0 μM and 0.8 μM were, respectively observed through TMB oxidation. It was proposed that Pt74Ag26 in MoS2-Pt74Ag26 nanohybrids provides a large surface area as well as abundant active sites for the effective adsorption of TMB molecules. Additionally, the lone-pair electrons present on the -NH2 groups of TMB molecule enhance the electron density and mobility in the MoS2- Pt74Ag26 nanohybrids for efficient reduction of H2O2 to H2O and oxidation of colorless TMB to blue colored oxidized TMB. Polymer-MoS2 composites were also found to exhibit glucose-sensing behavior through TMB oxidation. Yu et al. [127] synthesized polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP)-modified MoS2 NPs prepared via a facile one-pot hydrothermal technique. The detection limit of 320 μM and linear dynamic range of 103–104 μM were observed for glucose oxidation in the presence of TMB. However, the results were not fascinating as compared to other sensing materials (Table 5 ).

In addition to nanosheets, MoS2 with other morphologies are also found to exhibit horseradish peroxidase activities. Zhao et al. [128] synthesized MoS2 nanoparticles through a facile liquid exfoliation technique in an ethanol/water solvent system. Very low detection limits of 1.25 μM and 7 μM were observed for H2O2 and glucose, respectively. The oxidation of the glucose to gluconic acid, which releases H2O2, was catalyzed by glucose oxidation. The as-designed colorimetric sensor also showed excellent selectivity for glucose (Fig. 9 ). Zhao et al. [129] ascertained the role and importance of surface charges of MoS2 NPs on peroxidase-like activities. It was observed that negatively charged sodium dodecyl sulfate modified MoS2 nanoparticles showed better peroxidase-like activities as compared to positively charged cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) modified MoS2 NPs.

Yu et al. [130] modified hydrothermally prepared MoS2 nanoflakes with biocompatible surfactants like polyethyleneimine (PEI), polyacrylic acid (PAA), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), and cysteine (Cys) and compared the peroxidase-like catalytic activities. TMB and ABTS were used as chromogenic substrates. As compared to others, Cysteine-modified MoS2 nanoflakes showed better glucose-sensing behavior. The detection limit for glucose was 33.51 μM and the linear dynamic range varied from 50 to 1000 μM.

Some other metal sulfides in either modified or functionalized forms such as Hemin-functionalized WS2 nanosheets [131], functionalized ZnS NPs [132] and H2TCPP-CdS [133] are also reported in the literature for colorimetric glucose sensing using TMB as chromogenic substrate (Table 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mixed metal-oxide compounds based colorimetric biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: Mixed metal oxides as either composite or as spinel compounds have been also reported as efficient biosensor materials. These metal oxides may mimic the natural action of the horseradish peroxidase, glucose oxidase, or glucose dehydrogenase enzymes and result in non-enzymatic glucose oxidation directly or indirectly. Many spinels, inverse spinel and mixed spinel structures such as NiCo2O4 [134], CoFe2O4 [135], ZnFe2O4 [74], NiFe2O4 [76], etc. have shown the peroxidase-like activities. The process releases the H2O2 which further oxidizes TMB to oxidized TMB form (or other chromogenic substrates) supporting the colorimetric sensing of the glucose. Huang et al. [134] explored the peroxidase-like activity of the hierarchical spinel NiCo2O4 hollow sphere. For higher concentrations of glucose greater amount of H2O2 was produced and hence greater was the oxidation of the TMB to oxidized TMB (λmax = 652 nm). The absorbance varied linearly with the concentration of the glucose from 0.1 mM–4.5 mM. The system resulted in a low detection limit of 5.31 μM (Fig. 10 (a) and (b)). The related sensing mechanism is shown in Fig. 10 (c). The intrinsic peroxidase and oxidase-like activities of the spinel NiCo2O4 architectures were also confirmed by Su et al. [136] through electron spin resonance spectra for the oxidation of TMB by NiCo2O4 mesoporous spheres. The metal ions of the spinel through a series of partial electron exchange interaction produces hydroxyl free radicals (OH•) from the released and subsequently adsorbed H2O2 on the surface of the spinel. Fe3+ ions of the ZnFe2O4, NiFe2O4 and CoFe2O4 spinel have shown the tendency of the conversion of H2O2 into OH• radical (Eqs. (4)–(9)) [74–76].

NiFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles through the oxidation of ABTS were explored as a colorimetric biosensor for the detection of glucose within linear dynamic concentration range 0.94–25 μM and a limit of detection of 0.45 μM [76]. Improvement in the glucose-sensing behavior of the spinel structures is reported in combination with carbonaceous materials such as C3N4. Ngo et al. [137] reported MnFe2O4/g-C3N4 matrix for glucose analysis through a smartphone application with a high degree of accuracy. The high affinity of the MnFe2O4/g-C3N4 toward TMB and H2O2 released from glucose oxidation was calculated through Michaelis-Menten kinetics (Fig. 10 (d)). Better sensitivities were attributed to the symbiotic behavior between the MnFe2O4 and g-C3N4 for the intrinsic peroxidase-like activities which could catalyze H2O2 to form reactive OH• radical [138,139].

Other mixed metal compounds like MoS2-decorated MgFe2O4 [140], CoFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles [75], and chitosan-functionalized CoFe2O4 nanoparticles [141] are also reported as excellent colorimetric sensor materials for glucose sensing (Table 6 ).

Fluorophores are the molecules that absorb energy at specific wavelengths and re-emit energy at different wavelengths, and such characteristics are often referred to as local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) characteristics. This section reviews the various nanostructures materials such as gold/silver metal nanoclusters, metal oxides, mixed metal oxides, doped metal oxides, metal sulfides, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), lanthanide-doped nanomaterials, modified upconversion nanosystem, etc. are extensively studied fluorescence-emitting nano/micro materials. To explore the role of these nano/microscaled materials as a mimic for horseradish peroxidase enzyme, glucose oxidase is still required for the production of H2O2 during the oxidation of glucose to gluconic acid. Importantly, the fluorescence sensor does not require any chromogenic substrate to develop color for the visual detection of glucose. The fluorescent glucose sensor molecule must contain the glucose acceptor, donor fluorophore and fluorescent energy acceptor nearby [142]. Fluorescent probes reliably respond to the bioanalyte under certain particular optimized conditions. There are two ways of expressing the action of the fluorescent quenching. The interactions between the bioanalyte and the fluorescent probe may decrease the fluorescence intensity by quenching (off-mode) or enhance the fluorescence intensity by removing quenching effects (on-mode) [143]. In general, an “off-on” switch provides a higher signal-to-noise ratio than compared to “on-off” switch [144,145].

LSPR characteristics of the nano-sized noble metal nanoparticles such as Au or Ag make it possible the on-site efficient, fast and economic monitoring or screening of biologically important molecules through colorimetric assays. Studies have been conducted to control the photo-induced unique optical properties, free electron oscillations on metal surfaces, and LSPR properties leading to color changes in colorimetric sensing applications by novel metallic nanoparticles [146]. The basic principle of the LSPR is explained in detail elsewhere [147]. The LSPR properties are controlled by the distance between the nanoparticles i.e. aggregation ⇌ non-aggregation equilibrium of the metallic nanoparticles. For a distance smaller than the size of the metal nanoparticles LSPR band shifts to a longer wavelength (blue shift) [148,149]. Factors like size, shape, and dielectric environment of the metal nanoparticles also control the LSPR [150,151]. Au NPs with ∼ 20 nm particle size, have a wine-red color, the aggregation leads to a large red-shift of the local surface plasmon resonance accompanied by a characteristic color change from red to the blue [47,152]. However, Ag NPs are considered to be more advantageous over other Au NPs with comparable dimensions since Ag NPs possess higher extinction coefficients [153,154].

The LSPR characteristics can be enhanced by synthesizing the bimetallic nanoparticles and engineering their morphologies. Capping/labelling agents like l-cysteine [155,156], β-Cyclodextrin [157], cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) [158], 16-mercaptohexadecanoic acid (16-MHDA) [159], thiolated, methyl terminated ethylene glycol oligomers (thiol-PEG) [160], polyethyleneimine [161], DNA [162], etc (Table 7 ). attached to the metallic NPs further improve the glucose-metal NPs interactions. Such capping/labeling agents not only provide the adsorption sites for the analyte attachment but also stabilize the metal nanoparticle in solution with high ionic strength conditions for a longer time without any noticeable LSPR shifts at room temperature.

Chang et al. [47] have reviewed the different cross-linking, non-cross-linking, or destabilization aggregation of Au NPs in label-based colorimetric strategies. The shift in the LSPR on the attachment of the various agents is used to confirm the capping on the surface of the metallic nanoparticles. Au NPs with wine red solution showed λ max at 520 nm, while for orange-colored Au@Ag NPs solution, λ max was 375 nm [161]. Liu et al. [163] reported the deposition of a thin layer of Au shells on the Ag triangular nanoplates through epitaxial growth. The deposited thin layer offered a simple and efficient method for the colorimetric detection of glucose in human urine without using any glucose oxidase enzyme or organic chromophores as chromogenic substrates. It was proposed that Ag@Au core/shell triangular nanoplates were capable of oxidizing glucose. The H2O2 released from this oxidation etched the Ag triangular nanoplates and altered the LSPR of the Ag@Au core/shell triangular nanoplates. These LSPR characteristics led to significant measurable changes in the UV–vis. absorption peaks (Fig. 11 ).

In a similar study, Ag triangular nanoplates (Ag TNPs) synthesized using trisodium citrate and polyvinyl pyrrolidone by Cai et al. [164] were etched by H2O2 released from glucose oxidation in the presence of Au NPs as glucose oxidase (GOD) mimetic. The shape of the Ag TNPs changed from triangle to circle (Fig. 12 (a)). Since the tip sharpness and the aspect ratio of Ag TNPs significantly affect the LSPR band [165–167], any etching of the Ag TNPs would lead to a blue shift of the in-plane LSPR peak (∼630 nm) with significant absorbance decrease, which was detected in this case. The blue-shift of the LSPR peak wavelength was linearly proportional to the glucose concentration (Fig. 12 (b)). The morphology and size variations can be seen from the TEM images (Fig. 12 (c) and (d)). The actual distinct visual color change from dark blue to light pink for etched colorimetric assays for 10–80 μM concentration of glucose in 0.05 M trisodium citrate–citric acid buffer solution (pH = 6.6) saturated with O2 are shown in Fig. 12 (e).

Under similar experimental conditions, Ag nanoprisms were etched from the tips to rounded nanoplate morphologies by H2O2. Large LSPR blue shift of more than 120 nm with distinct color change from blue → purple → mauve was reported for glucose concentration in the range from 2.0 × 10-7 - 1.0 × 10-4 M [167]. Au NPs with an average diameter ∼ 4 nm were used as a mimic for glucose oxidase to catalytically oxidize glucose by Gao et al. [156]. Visual color changes from yellow to red were noticed due to the dissolution of Ag NPs by H2O2 released from glucose oxidation showed a linear dynamic concentration range and detection limit of 5–70 μM, and 3 μM, respectively. The concentration of Au NPs as a mimic for glucose oxidase affected the rate of dissolution of Ag NPs. An optimized concentration of 200 μL was found to exhibit maximum catalytic activity for fixed Ag NPs concentration at the absorbance density of 0.7 and glucose concentration of 30 μM in phosphate buffer with pH 7.0. Low catalytic activities below and above the optimized Au NPs concentrations were attributed to the fewer Au NPs concentrations than the substrate and interference between Ag NPs and Au NPs at high concentration, respectively.

Xu et al. [168] introduced glucose oxidase catalyzed etching of Au nanobipyramids (Au NBPs) for naked-eyed multicolor colorimetric assay for the detection of blood glucose. A longitudinal LSPR blue shift for Au NBPs was observed when the concentration of the glucose was increased from 0.0 μM to 200.0 μM (Fig. 13 (a)). In comparison, Au NBPs and Au NRs exhibited a detection limit of 0.02 μM and 10.0 μM, respectively. A linear dynamic range of 0.05–90 μM was observed for Au NBPs [168]. The linear fit equation was △λ = 2.68 [Glucose] +5.05 with R2 = 0.994 (Fig. 13 (b)). A distinct color change was observed for different concentrations of glucose (Fig. 13 (c)). The criterion was utilized to diagnose healthy and diabetic patients. Green or blue color indicated the normal blood sugar level i.e. 3.0–6.0 mM, while purple or pink color indicated high blood sugar level (approximately 7.0 mM) (Fig. 13 (d)). The color variations were similar to the one reported by Cai et al. [164] and Xia et al. [167].

Adnan et al. [155] reported the detection of glucose under ambient air conditions by monitoring the optical changes of Ag NPs and interaction between hydroxyl groups of glucose and the amine-carbonyl l-cysteine functional moiety capped on Ag NPs. Through these interactions, direct detection of glucose in the concentration range 0.01–0.17 μM with a detection limit of 1.2 × 10−4 μM was analyzed. The capping of the l-cysteine was ascertained by the shift in the sharp LSPR band near 400 nm in UV–Vis spectral window to low wavelength narrow SPR peak at 395 nm. Zhang et al. [157] reported the use of β-Cyclodextrin capped Ag NPs for detection of glucose based on the LSPR response shifts as a consequence of H2O2 produced from the enzymatic oxidation of glucose by glucose oxidase immobilized on a surface of Ag NPs. Ag NPs and graphene quantum dots composites loaded with glucose oxidase have also been used for the fabrication of colorimetric probes for glucose detection (Fig. 14 (a)) [169]. Direct correlation between the concentration of H2O2 and decrease in the Ag NPs LSPR at 425 nm in AgNPs/GQDs confirmed the etching of Ag0 to Ag + ions (Fig. 14 (b)). The linear regression equation for the concentration range 0.5 mM–8 mM was ΔA/A0 = (7.06087 ± 0.40925).Cglucose (mM) + (3.05473 ± 1.49321) with R2 = 0.97695 and LOD 30 μM (Fig. 14 (c)).

The concentration of the glucose not only affects the extent of etching and morphology of the Au or Ag NPs but also controls the rate of aggregation as well as surface charge properties. The average particle size for bare Au NPs was 22.5 ± 0.7 nm (-48 ± 0.41 mV), whereas on immobilization of glucose oxidase the particle size was increased to 158 nm (-21.4 mV). For solution containing 50 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL glucose, the particles sizes were 231.7 nm (-14.1 mV) and 1202 nm (-5.85 mV), respectively. These particle sizes and zeta potential variations resulted in the color change of the solution from red to blue [159]. The presence of some inorganic species like chloride (Cl-), bromide (Br-), iodides (I-) [170], molybdate ions (MoO4 2−) [171] and Fenton reagent (Fe2+-H2O2) [172] act as activators and thus can enhance the rate of the etching of metallic NPs with LSPR characteristics. Zhang et al. [158] used these ions in the presence of H2O2 to study the rate of etching of CTAB capped Au nanorods to visually detect glucose in analytic and real samples. It was proposed that glucose oxidase catalyzed glucose oxidation produces H2O2 which oxidizes I− to I2 in the presence of MoO4 2− ions. Au nanorods are easily etched by I2 at the tips along the longitudinal direction due to less surface passivation and higher reaction activities [171]. The change in the morphology of the Au nanorods is shown in the TEM images in Fig. 15 (a)-(c) (Inset show actual which color changes recognizable through naked eyes) and the corresponding blue-shift in LSPR peaks is shown in Fig. 15 (d). A naked eye color change was observed for even low glucose concentration of 3.0 μM (Fig. 15 (e)). For practical applications, the results were used for onsite detection of glucose in human urine sample. As compared to standard samples (Fig. 15 (f)), the color responses for healthy (Fig. 15 (g)) and diabetic patient (Fig. 15 (h)) were easily distinguishable.

One of the major issues related to etching-based glucose biosensors is the long etching duration which makes it less applicable for on spot real-time glucose sensing. Since H2O2 released from the oxidation of glucose is responsible for the release of OH• radicals, the detection time can be shortened by adding some known quantity of chemicals like Fenton reagent (Fe2+-H2O2) producing these radicals in the analyte solution. Fe2+ ions on reaction with H2O2 produce OH• radicals through a series of reactions (Eqs. (10)–(13)).

Liu et al. [172] realized the role of Fe2+-H2O2 reagent and significantly reduced the etching time from 12 h to 15 min and observed fast plasmonic shift for the Au nanorods during glucose detection. Saa et al. [170] compared the etching tendencies of the Cl-, Br-, and I- ions for Au nanorods. In contrast to Fe2+-H2O2, halide ions result in the formation of gold halides (AuX x
–) from the Au3+ ions formed by the etching. Since the oxidation trend for different halides is AuCl x
– < AuBr x
– < AuI x
–, the etching tendency followed the order Cl- < Br- < I-. As a result, in the presence of Cl- no oxidation of Au nanorods and shift in LSPR band was observed. However, the presence of I- ions resulted in fast oxidation of the Au nanorods even for a very low concentration of 20 mM.

One of the main disadvantages of novel metal-based etching colorimetric sensors is the irreversibility of the etching process, so the repeatability of such sensors is easily challenged. Besides, since the particle size of metal nanoparticles can affect the LSPR shift, appropriate control methods and optimal growth conditions are critical during the synthesis process.

Metal oxide nanomaterials with excellent optical, redox and enzyme-like activity have attracted researchers' attention to fabricate highly sensitive chromogenic substrate-free glucose sensors. The photoluminescence properties of the metal oxides are significantly affected by the presence of various quenching organic molecules. Considering these characteristics, fluorescence sensors have been fabricated utilizing different metal oxide nanomaterials such as MnO2, ZnO, TiO2, V2O5, etc. in pure form, doped form, or as composites with other optoelectronic materials. The use of metal oxide nanomaterials can also easily address the major drawbacks of the repeatability of metal-based etched colorimetric sensors. The use of MnO2 nanostructures in bare form or as composites has been reported recently since these materials are very easy to synthesis and yellow-colored MnO2 solutions show very strong absorbance in the range 300 nm–500 nm. Furthermore, two-dimensional MnO2 nanostructures are non-toxic and possess a large molar extinction coefficient of 9.6 × 103 M−1cm−1 [175]. Bare MnO2 nanosheets [176,177], MnO2 nanosheets modified with 5-carboxyfluorescein [178], MnO2 nanosheets/copper nanoclusters [179], MnO2-nanosheet-modified NaYF4:Yb,Tm@NaYF4 nanoparticles [180], MnO2 phenol-formaldehyde resin [181], etc. are reported in literature as highly efficient glucose biosensors (Table 8 ).

Zhang et al. [182] explored the glucose-sensing applications of flower-shaped MnO2 nanomaterial synthesized through the two-step hydrothermal method (Fig. 16 (a) and (b)). These flower-shaped MnO2 nanomaterials performed the role of an active nano-oxidizer as well as a color nano-indicator. The reaction between MnO2 and glucose was carried out in 250 mM NaOH at 37 °C for 50 min. A wide liner range from 10 μM to 5 mM and a low detectable limit of 10 μM was observed (Fig. 16 (c)). In the presence of glucose, the MnO2 nano-oxidizers are reduced to Mn2+, leading to the fading of the yellow color of MnO2 and hence the decrease in the absorbance (Eq. (14)) (Fig. 16 (d)). Two-dimensional yellow-colored MnO2 nanosheets with planar structures like graphene react with H2O2 to colorless Mn2+ ions [176]. The degree of color reduction can thus be quantified for the estimation of the concentration of H2O2. The results can further be validated by adding sodium or potassium periodates which oxidize Mn2+ to purple colored permanganate ions (
M n
O 4 −
) under acidic conditions (Eq. (15)).

To accelerate the oxidation of glucose to gluconic acid, enzyme glucose oxidase is also added in the analytical assay.

Sodzel et al. [183] reported about 90% quenching in photoluminescence with a 100 mM level of H2O2. For glucose oxidase immobilized ZnO nanoparticles linear dynamic range and detection limits were 10–130 mM and 10 mM, respectively. The very low bandgap of ∼3.3 eV between the conduction band and valence band is supposed to be responsible for such quenching behavior. Irradiation causes the excitation of electrons from the valence band to the conduction band. The carriers aim to recombine radiatively while emitting a photon with energy close to the ZnO bandgap (∼3.3 eV) for Near-Band-Edge emission (NBE) or through deep level defects with energies ∼2.5 eV for Deep-Level Emission (DLE), however in the presence of H2O2 released from oxidation of glucose to gluconic acid is converted into H2O and O2 on the surface of the ZnO assisted through conduction band electrons (Eqs. (16) and (17)). As a result, recombination is prevented which quenches the light emission intensity.
