ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal sulfides/tellurides based fluorescence glucose sensors
ce:section.ce:para: This category can be divided into two subcategories i.e. metal sulfides/tellurides quantum dots and metal sulfides/tellurides nano/microstructures. Class of metal sulfides and tellurides QDs is investigated for the spectrophotometric detecting of numerous bioanalytes. In general, the quenching process involves the direct transfer of electrons from the unitary, binary, or ternary QD systems to glucose oxidase which catalyzes the oxidation reactions of glucose releasing H2O2. The released H2O2 causes the quenching of the QDs leading to optical changes [185]. The QDs may act as transducers for bioanalytic sensing without any advanced conjugation with other chemicals, don't require and complex pretreatments, are destitute of the interference of autofluorescence and scattering light, show long-term photostability and allow real-time continuous monitoring [186,187]. In addition to these advantages, the very small size and hence the large surface-volume ratio of the QDs permits them to work as a fluorophore as well as multifunctional nanoscaffold for the binding of biomolecules [188,189]. Defects on the surface of the QDs and poor surface passivation are significant constraints causing the poor fluorescence yields. The issue of surface passivation was comprehended through the deposition of a capping layer (or shell) of inorganic and organic chemical moieties like 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) [185,190], mercaptosuccinic acid [191], glutathione [192], thiolate [193], thioglycolic acid (TGA) and 3-aminophenyl boronic acid (APBA) [194], etc (Table 9 ). The relatively simple “capping” procedure is found to be very effective in increasing the fluorescence yields [187,195].

The immobilization of some chemical moieties with attractive functional groups is also found to be progressively productive. The quenching effects of the H2O2 mar further be improved by photo induction. The process is generally referred to as photoinduced Electron Transfer (PIET) which results in the change in the room temperature phosphorescence (RTP).

Duong et al. [185] used hydrophilic surfactant 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) capped CdSe/ZnS core-shell QDs for the analytical sensing of glucose and discovered a direct correlation between the extent of fluorescence quenching and the concentration of the glucose. The quenching process was supposed to be due to the transfer of electrons from the QDs to enzymes like glucose oxidase and peroxidase which catalyze the oxidation/reduction reactions of glucose.

MPA-capped Mn-doped ZnS QDs (MPA: 3-mercaptopropionic acid) showed a detection limit of 0.0029 mM with two linear dynamic ranges from 0.005 to 0.1 mM and from 0.1 to 0.4 mM in Buffer solution with pH 7.4 through PIET causing RTP variations [190]. The conjugation of the glucose oxidase on the Mn-doped ZnS QDs was assisted through 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC)/N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) as coupling reagents were reported by Wu et al. [196] (Fig. 17 (a)). Glucose biosensing based on the effective quenching of the room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) of Mn-doped ZnS QDs resulted in improved enzymatic performance as was indicated by the Michaelis-Menten constant of 0.70 mM. Such conjugated QDs also showed better thermal stability in the temperature range of 20–80 °C. Low detection limit of 3 μM and two linear ranges from 10 μM to 0.1 mM and 0.1–1 mM were observed [196] (Fig. 17 (b), (c)).

The use of two different sized QDs with different fluorescence emission wavelengths in one nanoparticle is found to accomplish high sensitivity and lower detection limit leading to visual signal output observable through the naked eye. In such dual-emission QDs ratiometric fluorescence probes, one of the QDs acts as signal response unit while the other is used as a reference. Many binary and ternary QDs utilizing sulfides and tellurides such as CdTe QDs (r-QDs)/green-emitting CdTe QDs (g-QDs) [194], CdSe/ZnS (core/shell) QDs [197,198], CdTe/ZnTe/ZnS QDs (core/shell/shell) [199], CdTe/CdSe (core/shell) and CdSe/ZnTe (core/shell) [200], CdSe/CdTe/ZnTe (core/shell/shell) [201] etc. have been used as ratiometric fluorescence probes. By modifying the QD size and its chemical composition by engineering the core/shell features, fluorescence emission may be tuned from the near-ultraviolet to visible to near-infrared electromagnetic spectral range (Fig. 18 (a)) [186,187]. Depending upon the energies of the valence and conduction bands the core/shell QDs are further classified into Type-I and Type – II QDs (Fig. 18 (b)) [200]. In Type-I QDs, the charge carriers i.e. negatively charged electrons (e–) and positively charged holes (h+) are present in the core material where e– - h+ recombination occurs. In Type-II QDs, e– and h+ are confined in shell and core materials, respectively and e– - h+ recombination takes place at the core-shell interface.

Hao et al. [194] prepared the hybridizing red-emitting CdTe QDs (r-QDs) and green-emitting CdTe QDs (g-QDs) functionalized by aminophenylboronic acid (APBA). r-QDs were embedded in the SiO2 nanoparticle by the reverse microemulsion method while the g-QDs were attached to the surface of the SiO2 nanoparticle. Boronic acid and cis diol were used as a binder for the binding of the glucose g-QDs functionalized with APBA (Fig. 19 (a), (b)).
The fluorescence quenching of the of g-QDs resulted in continuous fluorescence color changes (from green to red) of the ratiometric fluorescence probe system [194] (Fig. 19 (c)).

Many catalysts are reported to influence the optical and quenching properties of the QDs. Azizi et al. [202,203] analyzed the catalytic applications of the ionic liquid, [(1,3-Pr2im)Br]/copper)] on chemiluminescence reactions of Mn-doped ZnS QDs and PL properties of the CdSe QDs. Generally, in the presence of [(1,3-Pr2im)Br]/copper)], PL emission intensity is increased due to the aggregation of the ionic liquids to behave as active surface agents. These agents are supposed to improve the rigidity of the CdSe QDs system and leading to an increase in PL intensity. Khataee et al. group [204,205] explored some nano/microstructured sulfides materials like WS2 nanosheets/Ag nanoclusters for the effective biosensing of the glucose through colorimetry, fluorometry, chemiluminescence technique.

However, the utilization of QDs in this field is as yet constrained because the photoluminescence quantum yield of the QDs diminishes essentially when they are changed from the hydrophobic to hydrophilic structure or when they are conjugated with some other materials. The QD nanoparticles can't be used under highly acidic or basic conditions since they undergo aggregation and precipitation. The optimized pH range of 6–10 is highly favorable for experimental measurements [185].

Carbon-based quantum dots can be either fluorescent in nature or fluorescent quenchers. Many carbon-based nanomaterials like graphene, graphene oxide, graphene quantum dots, carbon quantum dots, and carbon nanofibers, carbon nanotubes, etc. are reported as fluorescent glucose biosensors [206–213] (Table 10 ). Among these, carbon and graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes have been explored as biosensors owing to their efficient fluorescent nature originating due to excitation-dependent emission [214]. Carbon nanotubes are found to emit fluorescence in the near-IR range, which allow their applications in bio-imaging.

Conversely, graphene and graphene oxide nanomaterials act as fluorescent quenchers since they have a large π-electron plane. Non-radiative transfer of electronic excitation energy on the nanosheet plane leads to the fluorescence quenching through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). In the presence of glucose, the fluorescence is either turned on or turned off depending on the design of the sensor [215–217]. The graphene oxides with quantum size <100 nm, generally show superior properties to larger counterparts. This can be attributed to the high specific nanometer size effect and specific shape. Further, graphene oxide quantum dots are less toxic, biocompatible, more stable, show better fluorescence activities, have long-term resistance to photo-bleaching and the feasibility of functionalization at their edges as compared to carbon quantum dots [218–222]. Carbon nanodots with a diameter ranging from 4 to 10 nm, synthesized through a hydrothermal method showed a blue emission at 410 nm. These carbon nanodots underwent quenching by the H2O2 produced by the oxidation of glucose assisted by glucose oxidase and Fe2+ ions [223].

Wang et al. [224] explored the applications of the graphene quantum dots-based fluorescent bio-probe for glucose sensing through the quenching effect of graphene quantum dots. H2O2 formed during the glucose oxidation reaction catalyzed by glucose oxidase, ultimately oxidized phenol to quinone compounds, which was found to quench the fluorescence intensity of graphene quantum dots (Fig. 20 (a)). Further, the quenching fluorescence intensity was found to be proportional to the glucose concentration (Fig. 20 (b)). Some redox-modulated fluorescent strategies have also been developed using Fe2+/3+ redox couples for the detection of glucose using graphene quantum dots [225]. Both Fe2+ and Fe3+ are well-known electron acceptors and are sufficiently capable of rapid quenching of the fluorescence of semiconductor quantum dots [226,227]. The combination gave a linear dynamic concentration range of 0.1–30 μM with a very low detection limit of 0.021 μM for glucose sensing mediated through glucose oxidase. Further, the fluorescence intensity of graphene quantum dots/Fe2+/glucose oxidase system was gradually decreased with the increased concentration of glucose under the optimized reaction conditions. This was attributed to the faster reduction of Fe2+ to Fe3+ in the presence of H2O2 released from glucose oxidation. A higher amount of the Fe3+ ions in the reaction system caused greater fluorescence quenching of the graphene quantum dots [225].

The stability, fluorescence response, and long-term resistance to photo-bleaching can further be improved by introducing some other fluorophores or quenchers along with carbonaceous materials. Heteroatom dopants, metallic nanoparticles, metal oxides and some receptor molecules on the surface of the carbonaceous quantum dots can significantly endow new or improved structural, optical and physicochemical properties leading to improved sensing efficiencies. Ma et al. [228] supported carbon nanodots on silver nanoparticles (C-dots/AgNPs) and utilized these nanodots for the biosensing of glucose and H2O2. A three-stage strategy was proposed. The initial stage involved the quenching of C-dots’ fluorescence by AgNPs, followed by the production of H2O2 from glucose oxidation in the presence of glucose oxidase, while during the final stage H2O2 induced the etching of AgNPs to Ag + ions. This restores the C-dots’ fluorescence (Fig. 21 ). Hence, in the C-dots/AgNPs complex, AgNPs acted as a “nanoquencher” to decrease the C-dots’ fluorescence by surface plasmon-enhanced energy transfer (SPEET) from C-dots (donor) to AgNPs (acceptor).

Electron-deficient Lewis acid- Boron is also reported to exhibit bright blue fluorescence in aqueous as well as ethanol under the UV light for B-doped carbon quantum dots, in contrast to weak green light emission in the case of undoped carbon quantum dots. In contrast to other heteroatom dopants like O, N and S, it is difficult to dope the boron (B) atom in the framework of carbonaceous materials despite its comparable atom size to carbon. This may also be due to the significant difference in the electro-negativities of B (2.04) and C-atom (2.55) [229,230]. The excellent stability of the graphene materials due to the strong C-C bond also makes it difficult to replace C-atoms with B-atoms [231]. However, DFT studies conducted by Tsetseris et al. [232] indicated that B-atom can efficiently replace carbon atoms from the lattice, associated with a high activation energy barrier of about 1.3 eV. Shan et al. [233] observed a strong fluorescence emission peak at 368 nm for B-doped carbon quantum dots, while carbon quantum dots exhibited the emission band at 440 nm. The B-doped carbon quantum dots-based fluorescence sensor showed a linear response range of 8.0–80.0 μM with a very low detection limit of 8.0 μM for glucose.

Incorporation of some receptor molecules for glucose onto the carbonaceous sensor materials is profoundly favorable for better sensitivity. Many boronic acid derivatives have drawn significant attention as a molecular receptor for saccharides, especially glucose. The presence of two hydroxyl (-OH) groups offers high sensitivity for boronic acid derivatives-based glucose sensors. Boronic acid derivatives undergo complexation with glucose molecules which results in measurable changes in fluorescence properties. These changes also rely on the thermodynamic property of boronic acid derivatives to form a highly stable condensation complex with a cis-diol moiety whereas excluding the bonding with trans-diol moieties. Owing to this, boronic acid composites with other electron mediator carbonaceous species are used as fluorescent probes for glucose sensing. These probes may act as fluorophore or quencher depending upon the stuff with which boronic acid derivatives are mixed. The most commonly used derivative is phenylboronic acid. In addition to the aforesaid advantages, boronic acid derivatives-based biosensors are associated with a major limitation of low selectivity. However, this limitation can be overcomed by substituting the –OH groups of the boronic acid derivatives. The covalent interactions between boronic acid derivatives and the -OH groups of saccharides (favorable 1, 2-diols and unfavorable 1, 3-diols) form five- or six-membered rings, respectively that allow boronic acid derivatives to be a sensitive detector for saccharides (Fig. 22 ).

Qu et al. [234] functionalized graphene quantum dots with 3-aminobenzeneboronic acid and explored in vivo biosensing behavior for glucose in combination within the striatum of the rat. Extraordinary brightness and extremely high quantum yield for the selective sensing of glucose were observed. Further, the introduction of the 3-aminobenzeneboronic acid in graphene quantum dots improved the selectivity for glucose as compared to another saccharide like d-fructose, mannose, galactose, sucrose, lactose, and maltose. The presence of other hetero-atoms along with boronic acid derivatives in the carbonaceous matrix is supposed to further enhance the fluorophores/quencher behavior. 3-APBA-functionalized N-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probe have been developed by Jiang et al. [235] for the selective and sensitive detection of glucose. Nitrogen as a dopant efficiently induces charge delocalization and can also tune the work function of carbonaceous material [236]. At physiological pH and room temperature, the fabricated sensor exhibited a wide linear range of 1 mM–14 mM via fluorescence quenching effects. Shen et al. [237] presented a one-step “synthesis modification integration” strategy for the fabrication of a fluorescence probe for glucose using phenylboronic acid as the sole precursor for the modification of carbon dots (Fig. 23 (a)). The fluorescence intensities of the C-dots were sensitive to the glucose concentration at 408 nm (Fig. 23 (b)). A linear dynamic range of 9–900 μM with a 1.5 μM detection limit was recorded.

Similarly, Zuo et al. [238] also reported the synthesis of carbon dots by one step “synthesis-modification integration” strategy using 3-APBA and sodium citrate as precursors (Fig. 23 (c, d)). In another novel strategy, a nonenzymatic monosaccharide (glucose) detection was reported by fabricating carbon dots (C-dots) from linear sulfated polysaccharides κ-carrageenan and molecular receptor phenylboronic acid [239]. The phenylboronic acid present at carbon dots undergoes condensation to form a covalent bond with the –OH groups (cis-diols) of the glucose and results in the formation of boronate complexes. This bounding causes fluorescence quenching of the C-dots. A very low detection limit of 1.7 μM was observed for glucose without any significant interference of other bioanalytes.

Zhang et al. [240] in a contradictory approach observed an “aggregation-induced photoluminescence increasing” label-free glucose-sensing phenomena as compared to conventional “aggregation-induced quenching” as reported by other research groups [199,241,242] for in situ generated boronic acid groups on the boron-doped graphene quantum dots. It is attributed to the reaction of the two cis-diol units in glucose with the two boronic acid groups on boron-doped graphene quantum dots which form highly stable boron-doped graphene quantum dots−glucose aggregates, avoiding any intramolecular rotations and thus inducing an increase in the photoluminescence intensity.

Despite high selectivity and sensitivity, the fluorescence sensing techniques still encounter some major drawbacks including sophisticated and costly instrumentation for fluorescence measurements, time-consuming enzyme immobilizing process, complicated operation, significant signal drift, reversibility of the quenching process, repeating calibration, etc. [238]. These issues need redressal for the practical applications of the technique.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rare earth metal-doped vanadates as fluorescence glucose biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: Rare-earth ion doped materials with photoluminescent properties have been extensively used as a potential light source for field emission displays, plasma display panels, X-ray detectors, and white-light-emitting diodes [247]. In particular rare-earth ion-doped- rare-earth metal vanadates such as Eu3+ doped YVO4 [184,248], Gd3+ doped YVO4 [249], Sm3+ doped YVO4 [250], Dy3+ doped YVO4 [251], GdVO4:Ln3+ (Ln3+ = Eu3+, Dy3+ and Sm3+) [252] exhibit visible region photoluminescence due to the intra-configurational 4f-4f transitions. Additionally, tetrahedral VO4 3− ions also show absorbance in the UV region due to the transitions from 1A2 (1T1) ground state to 1A1 (1E) and 1E (1T2) excited states [253] along with charge transfer transitions from the filled 2p orbital of oxygen to the vacant d-orbitals of V5+ of VO4 - ion [254]. Further, these photoluminescent properties are also reported to be temperature-dependent.

YVO4 is a very significant rare-earth ion vanadate since its large single-crystal acts as an excellent polarizer and laser host material. Doping further improves its phosphor properties and luminescence quantum yield [251,255]. The doping of the rare-earth metal vanadates with rare-earth ions can be easily formulated due to similar ionic radii of trivalent ions as a result of lanthanide contraction and equal valence. However, GdVO4 and its co-doped congeners possess distinctly higher absorption cross-sections, high molar absorbance and quantum yield as compared to YVO4. After doping with Er3+/Yb3+, Ho3+/Yb3+, or Tm3+/Yb3+, Nd3+ GdVO4 materials also act as upconverters for emitting a range of emission colors. Especially, GdVO4 nanoparticles doped with Eu3+ or Er3+/Yb3+ are also reported to show a strong temperature-dependent fluorescence. Muhr et al. [184] prepared Eu3+-doped GdVO4 nanocrystals by precipitating the Gd3+(Eu3+)-citrate complex followed by conversion to the respective vanadate via dialysis. The nanocrystals showed red and largely red-shifted fluorescence under light excitation of about 300 nm, and the strongest peak appeared at 618 nm for 50% of Eu3+ doping. The presence of H2O2 strongly quenched the fluorescence of the 50% of Eu3+ doped- GdVO4 nanocrystals. The excitation peak wavelength of the Eu3+ doped-GdVO4 nanocrystals was dependent on the concentration of the Eu3+ contents. A blue shift from 325 nm to 295 nm was observed when the fraction of Eu3+ in the nanocrystals was increased from 5 to 100% in tris buffer with pH 7.4 (Fig. 24 (a)). This suggests that the Eu3+ concentration on the surface of the nanocrystals affects the quenching efficiency [184]. The normalized time trace measurements of the luminescence of 50% of Eu3+ doped- GdVO4 nanocrystals at 618 nm are shown in (Fig. 24 (b)). The addition of 50 nmol H2O2 and glucose after regular time intervals showed a brisk drop in the emission intensity. A very low detection limit of 1.6 μM was reported.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) for glucose biosensing
ce:section.ce:para: Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) is another spectrophotometric technique explored for glucose biosensing. The technique is also helpful in analyzing the molecular interactions within the living cells. It is well known that a high-energy excited fluorophore can relax back to the more stable ground state with lower energy either by emitting light, dissipating heat energy and/or by transferring the emitted energy to another fluorophore [256]. The later process forms the basis of the FRET technique. In this technique, two fluorophores are chosen in such a way that the emission wavelength of one of the fluorophore (donor fluorophore) overlaps with the excitation wavelength of the other fluorophore (acceptor fluorophore) [185]. The magnitude of FRET depends upon the extent of the spectral overlap and the distance between the donor and the acceptor fluorophore. A distance of <10 nm is highly favorable (Fig. 25 (a–c)) [142].

Many research groups have utilized semiconductor nanoparticle quantum dots, because of their appropriate size match and other characteristics, as luminescent inorganic fluorophores. The quantum dots are capable of efficient multicolor emission even with a single light source and hence are explored as donor fluorophores. Capping of the quantum dots with receptor molecules as well as some chromophores is found to improve the sensing behavior. The fluorescence quenching is observed in the presence of an acceptor fluorophore of appropriate characteristics due to FRET (Fig. 26 (a)). However, the structural and chemical modifications of the acceptor fluorophores prevent the FRET to occur and as a result, the semiconductor nanoparticle quantum dots start exhibiting fluorescence (Fig. 26 (b)). This characteristic is explored for efficient glucose-sensing since the enzyme-catalyzed oxidation of glucose to gluconic acid may alter the electronic features of the acceptor fluorophores. Hence the choice of appropriate fluorophores is a very important issue in glucose sensing through FRET. Several fluorophores receptors such as lectin concanavalin A, bacterial glucose-binding protein, boronic acid derivatives and enzymes like glucose oxidase, glucose dehydrogenase, hexokinase/glucokinase, nanoparticles like thiolated β-cyclodextrins (β-SH-CDs)-modified Au nanoparticles, etc. have been reported in the literature [257,258]. Many organic dyes including methylene blue [185], malachite green [259], carbocyanine dye [260], etc. are also conjugated with the receptor molecules for better FRET.

Rhee group [185] functionalized the CdSe/ZnS core-shell quantum dots with mercaptopropionic acid, glucose oxidase and horseradish peroxidase. Glucose sensing was formulated by the quenching of CdSe/ZnS core-shell quantum dots through FRET from quantum dots to the enzymatic reactions. However, comparatively high detection limits were recorded. NAD+-dependent glucose dehydrogenase (GDH) and CdSe/ZnS QDs or metallic AuNPs integrated hybrid systems are also reported for the detection of glucose through FRET [261]. CdSe/ZnS QDs functionalized with methylene blue (MB+)-coupled with NAD+-dependent glucose dehydrogenase (GDH) for the quantitative sensing of glucose through FRET. The GDH catalyzed oxidation of the glucose to gluconic acid, results in the reduction of the NAD+ to NADH. The reduced NADH is capable of further reduction of the MB+ to MBH. Further, the emission from the capped CdSe/ZnS QDs as donor fluorophore can excite the MB+ but not the MBH. Hence, in the absence of glucose, no fluorescence was observed from the capped CdSe/ZnS QDs as a result of FRET to the MB+. However, as soon as glucose was added to the system, no FRET occurs and as a result, capped CdSe/ZnS QDs start emitting fluorescence and the fluorescent intensity was increased with the increase in the concentration of the glucose. The detection limit of 10-5 M was observed. Chen et al. [259] designed a nanostructured biosensor including the donor fluorophore - CdSe/ZnS quantum dots, acceptor fluorophore-dextran-binding malachite green conjugated to concanavalin A-an enzyme with specific affinity to glucose. The quenched emission of CdSe/ZnS quantum dots was restored by displacing the dextran from concanavalin-A enzyme by glucose. Tang et al. [258] studied the FRET between the concanavalin A-conjugated CdTe quantum dots (QDs) as a donor and thiolated β-cyclodextrins (β-SH-CDs)-modified Au nanoparticles as acceptor fluorophores. A competitive approach was observed between the glucose and the β-cyclodextrins for the binding sites of the concanavalin A. Thus, the presence of the glucose prevented the FRET and the corresponding fluorescence intensity of the CdTe quantum dots was increased. For the as-designed nano-biosensor, a very low detection limit of 50 nm in serum was observed. Green CdTe QDs– concanavalin A conjugates donors and red CdTe QDs–d-(+)-glucosamine hydrochloride conjugates as acceptors fluorophores have also been used to form a FRET system [262]. Fluorescence quenching measured in the presence of glucose exhibited a linear calibration within 0.1–2.0 mM and a detection limit of 0.03 mM. Pre-attaching of glucose with the donor blocks the binding sites for the acceptor and thus blocks the FRET, which in turn prevents the fluorescence quenching of the quantum dots.

Coupling of the Tyramide reaction in the FRET process has been proposed by some research groups. The recent introduction of tyramide conjugates as substrates for horseradish peroxidase has emerged as a significant combination of enzyme and fluorescence probes for cytochemical analysis. In the presence of horseradish peroxidase enzyme, H2O2 is reduced to H2O while the phenolic part of tyramide is converted into a quinone-like structure having a radical on the C2 group at low concentration [263,264]. Conversely, at higher concentrations, during the labeling process, tyramide is converted into 2, 2′-dihydroxydiphenyl derivatives via tyramide radicals [260]. Usually, tyramide labeled with fluorescent probes is used as signal amplification fluorescent substrate for horseradish peroxidase-catalyzed reactions in immunoassay [265,266].

Huang et al. [267] efficiently analyzed the detection of H2O2, glucose, and uric acid based through FRET using Au nanoparticles as energy acceptors. Tetramethyl rhodamine-labeled tyramide and hydrogen peroxide catalyzed by horseradish peroxidase acted as donor fluorophore. The excitation energy of the donor fluorophore partially overlapped with the visible absorption bands of Au nanoparticles. It was noted that the quenching of the fluorescence signal varied linearly with the concentration of H2O2 (25–400 nM). Based on the principle for determination of H2O2, an assay of glucose and uric acid coupled with glucose oxidase (GOx)-mediated and uricase-mediated reaction, respectively. The same research group also explored the carbocyanine dye-labeled tyramide (Acceptor fluorophore) and H2O2 catalyzed by horseradish peroxidase-conjugated to 605 quantum dots (λ max. = 605 nm) (Donor fluorophore) composition for glucose sensing [260]. This was attributed to the overlap of the emission wavelength of the quantum dots with the absorption wavelength of the carbocyanine dye. Tyramide radical in the horseradish-catalyzed oxidation with directly used H2O2 (or produced by the glucose oxidation to gluconic acid) binds to the electron-rich functionalities of the tyrosine to form a tyramide-labeled horseradish peroxidase. A linearized positive co-relation between glucose concentrations (5.0 × 10−8–5.0 × 10−7 M) and Carbocyanine dye fluorescence intensity was observed. The corresponding detection limit was 5.0 × 10−8 M. Apo-glucose oxidase (the inactive form of glucose oxidase) modified Au nanoparticles as donor fluorophore and dextran labeled with fluorescein isothiocyanate (dextran-FITC) as acceptor fluorophore combination has also been reported for quantitative analysis of glucose in living cells. In the presence of glucose, quenched fluorescence of FITC was recovered as apo-glucose oxidase has a strong affinity for glucose over dextran. A low detection limit of 5 nM was observed for the assembled probe [268].

Despite excellent sensitivity and selectivity, the FRET techniques have some associated limitations such as the tedious process of immobilization of the fluorophore components, less stability of the coordinated enzymes, and multistep procedure for the fabrication of sensor probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and reusability of nano/micro-scaled materials based optical glucose biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: Most of the nano/micro-scaled materials based optical glucose biosensors cited herein showed excellent selectivity for glucose biosensing irrespective to the biosensing methods i.e. colorimetric, chromogenic substrate-free fluorescence or FRET. High selectivity of the biosensors involving glucose oxidase and peroxidase enzymes enable them to react to glucose only in the presence of variety of closely related compounds with structural similarities such as maltose, sucrose, galactose, fructose and mannose (Fig. 27 (a–d)). However, other interfering species like dopamine, ascorbic acid and l-cystine etc. have also been introduced and analyzed in assays for selectivity studies [56]. Glucose oxidase catalyzes the conversion of the glucose into gluconic acid along with the generation of H2O2. The selectivity of nano/micro-scaled materials based enzymatic optical glucose biosensors may be due to lock-key mechanism for enzyme–substrate interactions. The steric and electrostatic factors allow specific complementary substrate to bind to the active sites of the enzymes to form a transition state for the reaction [57–60]. Selectivity analysis is generally carried out in the presence of control experiments [62,67]. According to Dong et al. [68] magnetic GO–Fe3O4 nanocomposites exhibited excellent selectivity for glucose in the presence of lactose, maltose and fructose analogues even when the concentrations of such analogues were 10 times higher than that of glucose. Nonenzymatic GOx free glucose biosensors based on nano/micro-scaled materials, though are less selective as compared to enzymatic sensors. Efforts have been made to address the selectivity issues by forming hybrid composites.

Fluorescence etching based metallic sensors with LSPR characteristics, as explained in Section 4, offer opportunities to construct non-enzymatic colorimetric biosensors for glucose. l-Cystine/Ag nanoparticle [155,156], triangular Ag [164] and Au nanoplates and Ag@Au core/shell triangular nanoplates [163] are some examples to mention. Uncapped and capped metal sulfides and tellurides quantum dots (Table 9) and some carbonaceous materials-based fluorescence glucose biosensors (Table 10) were also found to mimic the glucose oxidase and peroxidase enzymes. The selectivity analysis of such fluorescence sensors is examined by monitoring the change in LSPR responses for glucose in the presence of common interfering species. Adnan et al. [155] reported negligible change in the LSPR intensity of l-cystine Ag NPs in the presence of interfering species such as ascorbic acid, sucrose and metal ions like Cu2+, Zn2+ and K+ ions. LSPR changes were attributed to the agglomeration of the Ag nanoparticles resulting in the formation of nano-aggregates in the presence of the glucose. Other interfering species thus do not significantly alter the interparticle interactions. Numerous studies mentioned excellent selectivity of the developed glucose biosensors, the detail mechanisms are still not very clear and need some in-depth comparative pre- and post-sensing analytical characterization of the biosensor materials.

Stability and reusability are yet other important issues related to the nano/micro-scaled materials based optical glucose biosensors. Enzymatic glucose biosensors suffer from poor stability due to the high sensitivity of the enzymes to the environmental changes. Aggregation of the nano/microstructures in the biosensing assays also reduces the surface area and hence the sensitivity of the biosensor. Oxidation of the glucose to gluconic acid also lowers the pH which not only deactivate the enzyme but also causes physico-chemical changes in the nano/micro-scaled materials [269].

The recent developments and diversification of nano/microstructured materials either in pure form or as composites have promoted the fabrication of optical glucose biosensors which offer many advantages including the simple equipment required, easy standard preparation, direct observation of color changes and hence can be used as analytic tools for glucose biosensing. The morphology, porosity, surface-volume ratio and composition of these nano/microstructured materials significantly affect the sensing parameters. Most of the colorimetric glucose biosensors use enzymes for the glucose oxidation and thus suffer from long term stability and reusability issues. Further, nano/micro-scaled materials generally undergo agglomeration in the biosensing assays during colorimetric biosensing. The presence of conducting polymers, amino acids with zwitter ion formation capabilities, porphyrins, supramolecules, biomolecules and different carbonaceous materials have been found to prevent the agglomeration of the nano/micro-scaled materials in optical biosensing, accelerate the electron transfer processes, and provide high adsorption capacities for the reactant substrates and target analytes. Etching based fluorescence sensors though are devoid of enzymes but suffer from a major limitation of irreversibility of the etching process. FRET-another spectrophotometric technique is advantageous in term of analyzing the molecular interactions, excellent sensitivity and selectivity but involves tedious process of immobilization of the fluorophore components, less stability of the coordinated enzymes, and multistep procedure for the fabrication of sensor probes.

Although great progress has been made for accurate and reliable glucose monitoring systems, there are still many challenges to overcome in the development of accurate systems in practical applications. Composite forms of the nano/microstructured materials can serve as transducers for the development and designing of non-invasive self-testing blood-glucose biosensors in near future.
Despite the remarkable progress made in glucose biosensor technology, there are still some challenges in achieving reliable glucose monitoring.

One of the major drawbacks in colorimetric biosensing of the glucose is that the nano/microstructures mainly mimic the peroxidase enzyme and glucose oxidase enzyme is still required for the oxidation of the glucose to gluconic acid. The environmental factors like temperature, pH and products formed during oxidation of glucose significantly affect the biological activities of the enzymes. These factors reduce the sensitivities of the nano/micro-scaled materials based optical glucose sensors. The future research should thus focus on inducing glucose oxidase like activities and further improvement in the peroxidase like activities of the nano/micro materials through tuning the surface properties of the nanomaterials by capping and surface modification, doping with noble nanomaterials and forming composites. Additionally, stability and reusability issues associated with nano/micro-scaled materials based optical glucose biosensors also need redressal for practical applications.

The standardization of methods for synthesizing novel nano/microstructured materials in pure form or composite form has not yet been achieved. From a variety of nano/microstructured composites reported is difficult to compare the different parameters required for significant glucose biosensing applications.

From the viewpoint of manufacturing and commercialization, glucose sensors based on nano/microstructured materials should be cost-effective, reliable, stable, easy to operate and capable of mass production to ensure routine self-monitoring. Further, method and composition calibration of the glucose biosensors based on nano/microstructured materials is still an untouched area of research. Such calibration and quality control are highly desired to achieve good excellent glucose biosensing characteristics in the physiological range. Additionally, selectivity is another issue that needs considerable attention to achieve real sample analysis.

The toxicity and other hazardous characteristics of nano/microstructured materials to humans and the additional impact on the environment are rarely addressed. These effects must be evaluated to ensure to popularize the nano/microstructured materials for the development of portable glucose biosensors.

Compared with other traditional invasive glucose sensing technologies, optical biosensing represents a promising and accurate glucose analysis technology. Thus, future research should be more focused on manufacturing and designing some optical non-invasive glucose biosensors.
